name: Danti
usesInAyurveda: Tridoshic Modulation: Prominently pacifies Kapha and Pitta doshas. Due to its Tikta (bitter) and Katu (pungent) Rasa and Ushna (heating) Virya, it is the most prestigious herb for 'Virechana' (purgation) and 'Shothahara' (anti-inflammatory) therapies, clearing the sticky and heavy qualities of Kapha. It may increase Vata in excess due to its sharp action.
Karma (Actions): Virechaka (purgative), Shothahara (anti-inflammatory), Deepana (appetizer), Pachana (digestive), Krimighna (anthelminthic), and Vishaghna (anti-toxic).

API/AFI Adherence: The dried root is the official medicinal part as per the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API). It is a primary ingredient in iconic classical formulations like Danti-Arishta, Dantir-haritaki, and various medicated oils for localized swelling.
Specific Indications: Used in Udara-roga (ascites/abdominal enlargement), Kamala (jaundice), Arsha (hemorrhoids), Ashmari (kidney stones), and chronic inflammatory skin conditions.
usageForm: Purified root powder (Churna), Root decoction (Kwatha), Medicated ghee (Ghrita), Fermented preparation (Arishta/Asava).
mainMedicinalUses: Expert Purgative Agent: Danti is a potent 'Virechana' (purgative) drug in Ayurveda. It is used to clear systemic toxins and excess Pitta from the body by inducing controlled purgation. WHO-aligned research recognizes its role in clearing intestinal stagnation and managing severe constipation.

Ascites and Abdominal Dropsy: Historically known as the premier remedy for 'Udara-roga' (abdominal enlargement). It effectively stimulates the elimination of excess fluid from the peritoneal cavity through its systemic cleansing action.

Liver and Spleen Detox: Encourages healthy bile secretion and protects liver cells from inflammatory toxins. It is traditionally used in 'Kamala' (jaundice) management to clear the 'Pitta-Avarana' (Pitta-blocking) conditions in the liver channels.

Dermatological Excellence: Widely used for its ability to treat chronic skin conditions (Kustha) and localized swellings (Shotha). A paste made from purified roots is traditionally applied topically to reduce hard swellings and promote the healing of chronic boils.

Anthelminthic and Gut Support: Acts as a powerful 'Krimighna' (de-worming) agent, especially effective against intestinal parasites and worms that thrive in a high-Kapha environment. It clears the gut lining of 'Ama' (toxins).
Joint Pain and Inflammation: Its systemic anti-inflammatory (Shothahara) properties help in reducing localized pain and swelling in conditions like rheumatoid arthritis (Amavata) and chronic gout.

Phytochemical Profile: Rich in diterpenoids (baliospermin, montanin), saponins, flavonoids, and various resins that provide its multifaceted medicinal benefits, particularly its potent anti-oxidant and pro-kinetic properties.
plantProfile: Unique History: Classified under the 'Virechana' (purgative) and 'Shothahara' (anti-inflammatory) groups in ancient Ayurvedic treatises like Charaka Samhita. It is spiritually associated with 'Tiksna-shodhana' (sharp-purification). It is noted as the 'Wild-Croton' due to its powerful purgative action.

Physical Characteristics: A perennial, monoecious undershrub growing up to 1-2 meters in height, with numerous branches and alternate, ovate-lanceolate leaves with toothed margins. It features small, greenish-yellow flowers and hairy, globose capsules containing three seeds. The root is thick, long, and brownish-grey with a pungent taste. It is found in deciduous forests and sub-Himalayan regions throughout India.

Specific Chemical Compounds: Primarily contains Diterpenoid esters (Baliospermin, Montanin), Saponins, and Resins. The presence of these diterpenoids is crucial for its pro-kinetic, anti-microbial, and cytotoxic action on localized tumors.

Strict Standardizations: The root is standardized in API based on macroscopic features (tough bark, brownish core). Physicochemical benchmarks: total ash (<10%), alcohol-soluble extractive (>5%), and water-soluble extractive (>12%). Identification via HPTLC is used to monitor specific marker diterpenoids.

Purification Process (Shodhana): The roots are traditionally wrapped in 'Pippali-paste' (Piper longum) and heated to undergo 'Paka' before use, to reduce its excessive harshness and potential for causing intestinal cramping.
preparationMethods: Purified Root Powder (Churna): 1 gram to 2 grams of fine powder taken once daily with warm water or buttermilk (as an absorbent Anupana) for purgation.
Danti-Arishta: 15-30ml of fermented extract taken twice daily after meals for managing liver and spleen disorders.
Root Decoction (Kwatha): 5g of purified root boiled in 100ml water reduced to 25ml for chronic ascites and abdominal congestion.
Medicated Ghee: The root is processed with cow's ghee to treat chronic inflammatory skin issues through its systemic 'Virechana' quality.
External Paste: Ground purified root or paste applied directly to chronic non-healing ulcers and boils to reduce localized swelling and heat.

dosages.Children: Generally NOT recommended for children under 12 due to its potent purgative action. Smallest doses (125-250mg) only under strict expert guidance.
dosages.Adults: Powder: 1 gram to 2 grams; Decoction: 20-50 ml; Arishta: 15-30 ml, divided throughout the day before meals.
dosages.Elderly: Very low doses (125-250mg) are advisable initially, monitoring for any intestinal cramping or severe dehydration.

safetyProfile: Contraindications: Strictly contraindicated in individuals with active high-Pitta (burning sensation, hyperacidity, stomach ulcers) and during pregnancy as it is a potent uterine stimulant. Avoid in extreme Vata (severe constipation with dryness).
GRAS Status: Classified as a safe medicinal herb ONLY when properly purified (Shodhana) and taken in recommended therapeutic dosages under practitioner guidance.
Herb-Drug Interactions: Monitor if taken alongside synthetic anti-diabetic or anticoagulant medications as it may potentiate their effects. No severe adverse interactions reported in WHO monographs.
Adverse Effects: Overdose or use of non-purified roots results in severe abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, and dehydration. Discontinue use if cramping occurs.
